coredata.dc:title: Double responsive analysis of carbaryl pesticide based on carbon quantum dots and Au nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yuye
Qin, Xiu
Yuan, Chunling
Shi, Rui
Wang, Yilin
coredata.dc:description: B, N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with an emission peak at 490 nm was prepared by a hydrothermal method. It was found that the fluorescence of CQDs could be effectively quenched by Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) with the absorption peak at 520 nm. Thiocholine (TC), which was produced from acetylcholine (ACh) hydrolysis catalyzed by acetylcholinesterase (AChE), could induce the aggregation of AuNPs along with a drop in the absorption at 520 nm and result in the recovery of quenched CQDs fluorescence. When carbaryl was introduced, which inhabited the catalytic activity of AChE, thus the aggregation of AuNPs and the recovery of CQDs fluorescence was decremented. On the basis of above phenomena, we established a colorimetric and fluorescent double reading method for carbaryl determination. Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence response was strongly correlated with carbaryl concentration in the 0.20–150 μg/L range, and the absorbance response was linearly proportional to the concentration of carbaryl in the range of 0.2–20 μg/L. This method was used to the determination of carbaryl in tap and lake water samples with high precision and accuracy, indicating the potential of the method in practical applications with simple operation, good accuracy and high sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Gold nanoparticle
Fluorescence
Colorimetry
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CQDs fluorescence quenching induced by AuNPs
The feasibility of dual signal detection of carbaryl
Selection of detection conditions for carbaryl
Application in the analysis of water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (inset) size distribution of AuNPs. (B) TEM image and (inset) size distribution of CQDs.
(A) Normalized fluorescence and absorbance spectra. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs excited at 365–445 nm.
(A) The fluorescence spectra of CQDs in the presence of various AuNPs concentrations. (B) Plots of the fluorescence intensity ratio vs. AuNPs concentration.
(A) The absorption spectra of AuNPs at different system. (B) The florescence spectra of CQDs at different system. [AChE] = 5 mU/mL, [ATC] = 10 μM, [AuNPs] = 3.5 nM, [carbaryl] = 150 μg/L.

(A) The fluorescence spectra CQDs-AuNPs-ATC-AChE with different concentration of carbaryl ranging from 0.0 to 150 μg/L. (B) The linear plot of F I/F I0 versus the logarithm of carbaryl concentrations (0.2–150 μg/L). (C) The absorption spectra of AuNPs in the presence of different concentration of carbaryl in the range of 0.0–150 μg/L. (D) The linear plot of A 650/A 520 versus the logarithm of carbaryl concentrations (0.2–20 μg/L) [AChE] = 5.0 mU/mL, [ATC] = 10 μM, [AuNPs] = 3.5 nM.
(A) Fluorescence and (B) absorbance response of other substances with the concentration of 2000 μg/L toward the CQDs-AuNPs-AChE-ATC system. [AChE] = 5 mU/mL, [ATC] = 10 μM, [AuNPs] = 3.5 nM.
Schematic diagram of dual signal analysis of carbaryl.
Comparison of the present method with other reported analysis methods.
Detection of carbaryl in water samples. (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Double responsive analysis of carbaryl pesticide based on carbon quantum dots and Au nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Nanning, 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Nanning, 530004, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory for Agro-Environment and Agro-Product Safety, Nanning, 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory for Agro-Environment and Agro-Product Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory for Agro-Environment and Agro-Product Safety, Nanning, 530004, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Nanning, 530004, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: B, N-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with an emission peak at 490 nm was prepared by a hydrothermal method. It was found that the fluorescence of CQDs could be effectively quenched by Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) with the absorption peak at 520 nm. Thiocholine (TC), which was produced from acetylcholine (ACh) hydrolysis catalyzed by acetylcholinesterase (AChE), could induce the aggregation of AuNPs along with a drop in the absorption at 520 nm and result in the recovery of quenched CQDs fluorescence. When carbaryl was introduced, which inhabited the catalytic activity of AChE, thus the aggregation of AuNPs and the recovery of CQDs fluorescence was decremented. On the basis of above phenomena, we established a colorimetric and fluorescent double reading method for carbaryl determination. Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence response was strongly correlated with carbaryl concentration in the 0.20–150 μg/L range, and the absorbance response was linearly proportional to the concentration of carbaryl in the range of 0.2–20 μg/L. This method was used to the determination of carbaryl in tap and lake water samples with high precision and accuracy, indicating the potential of the method in practical applications with simple operation, good accuracy and high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), one of the most popular representatives of fluorescent nanomaterials, have been widely used in a variety of fields ranging from fluorescent analysis [1–3] to bioimaging [4,5] to light emitting diode [6,7], due to their unique properties such as strong fluorescence, low cost, non-toxic, and good biocompatibility [8]. As members of metal nanomaterials, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have also attracted extensive attention. Many reporters have been published, in which AuNPs were used for the determination of various analytes [9–12]. For example, colorimetric method based on analyte-induced aggregation of AuNPs has been used to detect low content metal ions [10], amino acid [11], glucose [12], etc. For their excellent fluorescence quenchers, AuNPs have been constructed as numerous off-on fluorescence probes and fluorophors for the detection of melamine [13], biothiols [14], dopamine [15], protein [16], pesticide [17], and so on.

Carbamate pesticides (CPs), a synthetic pesticide developed after organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), are widely used in modern agricultures because of their high insecticidal and poor persistence in natural conditions [18,19]. However, the residues of these pesticides also trigger a series of problems, such as the pollution of water resources, causing certain harm to human health [20,21]. It is found these pesticides can inactivate acetylcholinesterase (AChE), leading to the accumulation of acetylcholine (ACh) and damaging the human immune system [22]. Therefore, sensitive determination of carbamate pesticides in real samples is of significant importance for human health. Over the past decade, many analytical methods have been established for the detection of pesticides in various samples, like high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [23], liquid/gas phase chromatography–mass spectrometry (L/GC-MS) [24], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [25,26], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [27], electrochemical analysis [28,29], colorimetry [30], and fluorescence method [31,32], etc. Among them, HPLC, L/GC-MS, and SERS need expensive instruments, complicated sample pretreatment and professional operators. ELISA requires expensive biological reagents. Compared with above techniques, colorimetry and fluorescence analysis possess the advantages of simple operation and low cost. Under the catalysis of AChE, acetylthiocholine is easily hydrolyzed to generate thiocholine (TC) with positive charges, which can replace citrate on the surfaces of negatively charged AuNPs via electrostatic interactions and Au-SR (R: choline) bond. Therefore, the citrate-capped AuNPs aggregate, the color of AuNPs solution changes from red to purple, and the characteristic absorption peak of AuNPs at about 520 nm decreases in intensity. Based on these, Zhang et al. [33] established a colorimetric method for the assay of AChE activity and its inhibitor (tacrine) in 2009. Then, the double signal (colorimetry and fluorescence) analysis method based on CQDs-AgNPs [34] and rhodamine B-AuNPs [35] were used for the highly sensitive determination of AChE activity and pesticides (CPs and OPs). A fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) probe composed of AuNPs and CQDs has recently been used for OPs determination [36,37].

In this work, CQDs with emission peak at 490 nm were prepared by a hydrothermal method, and AuNPs with absorption peak at 520 nm were prepared via reduction of chloroauric acid by sodium citrate. Due to the effective overlapping of the two spectra, the fluorescence of CQDs could be quenched by AuNPs through the inner filter effect (IFE). As illustrated in Scheme 1 , under the catalysis of AChE, acetylthiocholine iodide (ATC) is hydrolyzed to produce thiocholine (TC) [38], which induces the aggregation of AuNPs along with a decrease in absorption at 520 nm (Fig. S1), thus the quenched fluorescence of CQDs is recovered. When carbaryl is introduced, the activity of AChE is inhibited, avoiding the production of TC and the aggregation of AuNPs, and the recovery of quenched CQDs fluorescence is reduced. Therefore, by measuring the fluorescence of CQDs and the absorbance of AuNPs, a double signal assay method for the detection of carbaryl was established. To the best of our knowledge, the CQDs-AuNPs as dual-signaling probe for carbaryl pesticide have not been reported. Furthermore, compared with the FRET-based probe, the IFE-based probe does not need the intermolecular interaction between fluorophore and quencher, which is simple and convenient [39].

AChE (500UN from Electrophorus Electricus), acetylthiocholine iodide (ATC), carbaryl, and chloroauric acid were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, trisodium citrate, p-phenylenediamine, borax, FeSO4, NaCl, Na2SO4, and KCl were obtained from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. All of these reagents were analytical reagent grade and used as received.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showing the size and morphology of CQDs and AuNPs were achieved with a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN TEM instrument (FEI Company, America) operating at 200 kV. Zeta potential was performed on a Zeta Sizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CQDs was conducted with Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo, America). The fluorescence spectra of CQDs were recorded on a Shimadzu 5301 PC fluorospectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan), and Unico 4802 Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China) was applied to obtain UV–Vis absorption spectra. The centrifugation was performed on an H1850 high speed centrifuge (Changsha, China).

CQDs were prepared according to the literature [40] with some modifications. In brief, 0.5 g of each citric acid, p-phenylenediamine, and borax were thoroughly mixed with 40 mL of DDW in a 100 mL beaker. The mixture was then transferred into a 75 mL autoclave and kept in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was purified with a 0.22 μm microfiltration membrane. Then the collected solution was further purified by dialysis (MWCO = 1000) against DDW for 24 h. Finally, the obtained CQDs were put into a 250 mL volumetric flask and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C ready for use.

The AuNPs with a particle size of 13 nm were prepared via sodium citrate reduction of chloroauric acid [36]. In a 250 mL beaker, 100 mL of 1 mM HAuCl4 was heated to a rolling boil and then added with 10 mL of 38.8 mM trisodium citrate under vigorous stirring. After the reaction continued for 10 min, a wine-red AuNPs colloid solution was obtained. After the solution cooled down room temperature, it was diluted and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further use. According to the absorption and molar extinction coefficient of AuNPs at 520 nm, the AuNPs concentration was calculated to be 10 nM with the Beer's law.

First, 50 μL of AChE solution (5 mU/mL) and 100 μL of carbaryl with various amount were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, 700 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.5, 10 mM), 100 μL of CQDs, 700 μL of AuNPs (3.5 nM), and 100 μL of ATC (10 μM) were added into the mixture. After well mixed and incubated at 37 °C for another 15 min, the fluorescence emission and the UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixture were recorded at room temperature, respectively.

The morphology and particle size of AuNPs and CQDs were characterized using TEM. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, the obtained AuNPs are spherical in shape, well dispersed and uniform in size. The scale bar is 20 nm, indicating that the diameter of AuNPs is about 13 nm. Fig. 1B indicates that the CQDs are monodisperse spherical with a particle size ranging of 2.2–4.5 nm, and the calculated average diameter is about 3.2 nm, which is consistent with the literature [40]. By using rhodamine 6G as a fluorescence standard [41], the calculated fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CQDs is 30%, indicating the CQDs can be used as a highly sensitive fluorescent probe.

The formation of AuNPs was further confirmed by UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 2 A, with a surface plasmon resonance characteristic absorption peak of gold nanoparticles at 520 nm, which is consistent with that reported in the literature [42]. Fig. 2B depicts the florescence spectra of CQDs when excited from 365 nm to 445 nm. It can be seen that the maximum emission intensity is obtained when the excitation wavelength is 385 nm. As shown in the figure, when the range of excitation wavelength is between 385 and 445 nm, the emission peak only shifts from 489 nm to 507 nm. The subtle changes in the emission peak could be ignored. The excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior might be due to the following reasons: (1) the less surface defects and the narrow particle size distributions of B, N-doped CQDs [43], and (2) The B–N bonds are formed, leading to the replacement of C atoms in the aromatic structure. As boron is an electron deficient element, which has one electron less than carbon, the free electrons from the N atoms are no longer delocalized throughout the aromatic domain, but rather localized in the B–N bonds [44].

FTIR spectra were used to analyze the surface state of CQDs (Fig. S2). The peaks at 3369 and 2932 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of O/N–H and C–H, respectively. The peak at 1575 cm−1 is attributed to CC stretching vibration. Peaks at 1695, 1514, and 1298 cm−1 are associated with the stretching type of N–CO, CN, and C–N–C bonds, respectively. The FTIR peak at 1398 cm−1 is translated into B–O stretching. Thus, N and B atoms are successfully doped into the CQDs as evidenced from the FTIR data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs fluorescence quenching induced by AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching refers to any process that decreases the fluorescence intensity of a fluorophor and it is a result of a variety of molecular interactions, which has been widely used in the development of various fluorescence sensing technologies. The quenching mechanism includes fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching, dynamic quenching, etc. Because of the high extinction coefficient of the AuNPs at 520 nm (2.7 × 108cm−1M−1) [36], they have been widely used as absorbers. A large overlap exists between the CQDs emission spectra and AuNPs absorption spectra (Fig. 2A), suggesting that FRET or IFE may occur. The zeta potential measurement of CQDs is −10.55 mV (Fig. S3), indicating the CQDs are negatively charged. AuNPs are negatively charged with a zeta potential of −35.89 mV. Since both of them are negatively charged, the distance between the two substances is too large to satisfy the condition of FRET because of the electrostatic repulsion between them. As can be seen in Fig. S4, the absorption spectra of AuNPs and AuNPs-CQDs mixture almost overlap with each other, indicating the non-existence of an AuNPs-CQDs complex in the ground state and the absence of static quenching. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs with various AuNPs concentrations were recorded and the results are shown in Fig. 3 A. It can be observed that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs decreases gradually with the increase of AuNPs concentration. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence intensity ratio and the AuNPs concentration was fitted to the Stern–Volmer equation, F Q0/F Q = 1+K sv[AuNPs], where F Q0 and F Q were the emission intensity of CQDs in the absence and presence of AuNPs, respectively. The quenching constant (K sv) was found to be 8.76 × 108 M−1 calculated from the slope of the regression curve. Such a high K sv value indicates the existence of dynamic quenching mechanism [45]. The above experimental results suggest that the fluorescence of CQDs was effectively quenched by AuNPs via both IFE and dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of dual signal detection of carbaryl
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the proposed dual-readout strategy for carbaryl detection, we studied the inhibition effect of carbaryl on AChE and recorded the fluorescence and absorption spectra. The characteristic absorption peak of the well-dispersed AuNPs located at 520 nm (curve a in Fig. 4 A). When CQDs and AuNPs were incubated, the absorption spectra (curve b in Fig. 4A), in contrast to pure AuNPs solution, remained unchanged. However, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 490 nm (curve a in Fig. 4B) dramatically decreased (curve b in Fig. 4B) due to IFE. After ATC and AChE were incubated with CQDs-AuNPs mixture, the absorbance of AuNPs at 520 nm declined significantly (curve c in Fig. 4A). Furthermore, the quenched fluorescence of CQDs recovered (curve c in Fig. 4B). It is worth noting that the surface plasmon resonance absorption band at about 650 nm in the absorption spectra of the aggregated AuNPs [46] did not appear in this study, which is consistent with the literatures [35,39]. To investigate the influence of carbaryl on AChE, we first incubated carbaryl with AChE for 30 min at 37 °C. Tris-HCl buffer, CQDs, AuNPs, and ATC were then added sequentially and allowed to react for another 15 min. We found that the absorbance at 520 nm of AuNPs recovered remarkably (curve d in Fig. 4A), and the fluorescence recovery of CQDs was impeded (curve d in Fig. 4B). These results showed that AChE was deactivated in the presence of carbaryl, which prevented the generation of TC from the AChE-catalyzed hydrolysis of ATC. Consequently, the presence of carbaryl could retard the decrease of the absorption peak of AuNPs at 520 nm and the fluorescence recovery of CQDs at 490 nm. Thus, we can use the double-signal system to detect carbaryl in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of detection conditions for carbaryl
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, the quenching efficiency of CQDs is defined as [1− (F Q/F Q0)]. We calculated the quenching efficiency with various amounts of AuNPs (Fig. S5A). About 75% quenching efficiency is observed while the AuNPs concentration is up to 3.5 nM. However, the further increase of AuNPs concentration only gives rise to a slow increase of fluorescence quenching degree. Therefore, 3.5 nM AuNPs was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

Enzymes play an important role in the system by catalyzing the hydrolysis of ATC to produce TC and facilitating the aggregation of AuNPs to restore fluorescence. To ensure the maximum analytical response from the CQDs-AuNPs-ATC-AChE system, the optimized experiment conditions including pH of buffer, reaction temperature and concentration of AChE were performed. ΔF=F R-F R0 is used to evaluate the fluorescence recovery efficiency, where F R and F R0 denote the emission intensity of the CQD-AuNPs with or without ATC and AChE at 490 nm, respectively. As one of biological enzymes, AChE’ s activity is highly pH dependent, which could be denatured under extreme pH conditions. Hence, the influence of pH value ranging from 6.5 to 9.0 on the recovery effect was investigated. As shown in Fig. S5B, the fluorescence recovery (ΔF) first enhances and then decreases with the increment of pH value, the maximum ΔF is obtained at pH = 7.5, which is chosen for the further experiments. The effects of incubation temperature at the range of 25–50 °C on ΔF are also studied and the experiment results are shown in Fig. S5C. An increase in incubation temperature from 25 to 37 °C leads to the increased fluorescence recovery whereas a further increase in incubation temperature from 37 to 50 °C results in a great decrease, incurring the inactivation of AChE at high temperature. The influence of AChE concentration was examined (Fig. S5D). A higher AChE concentration enables the substrate ATC to hydrolyze more completely and produce more TC. However, the increase of ΔF slows down obviously when the AChE concentration exceeds 5.0 mU/mL. Therefore, the best response conditions are as follows: pH 7.5, incubation temperature 37 °C, and AChE concentration 5.0 mU/mL.

Under the optimized conditions, a double-signal response of carbaryl was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 490 nm decreases gradually with the increase of carbaryl concentration. In accordance with the corresponding fluorescence response, we calculated the inhibition ratio of carbaryl, defined as F I/F I0, where F I and F I0 are the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-AuNPs-AChE-ATC system in the presence and absence of carbaryl, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5B, a good linear relationship is found between F I/F I0 and the logarithm of carbaryl concentration in the range of 0.2–150 μg/L. The regression equation could be described as F I/F I0 = - 0.151LogC + 0.909 with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.99. The limit of detection (LOD) based on 3Sb/k for five measurements of the blank was 0.06 μg/L, which is much lower than the environmental quality standards for surface water of China (50 μg/L) (GB 3838−2002), indicating that this method has a high sensitivity.

Fig. 5C exhibits UV–vis absorption spectra dependent on the concentration. With increasing carbaryl concentration, the absorbance of AuNPs at 520 nm increases gradually, along with the decreasing absorption band between 600 and 800 nm. As shown in Fig. 5D, a good linear relationship is observed between the value of A 650/A 520 (the ratio of absorbance at 650 and 520 nm) and the logarithm of the carbaryl concentration in the range of 0.2–20 μg/L. The regression equation is A 650/A 520 = −0.187LogC+0.501 with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.99.

A comparison of the analytical performance of this method with other established methods is summarized in Table 1 . The LOD of our method was lower than that obtained by other detection methods. The linear range of our proposed method was wider than that of some of these methods. Thus, this dual-mode method has high sensitivity and accuracy for detecting carbaryl.

To explore the selectivity of the dual-signal detection for carbaryl, the fluorescence and absorbance responses of CQDs-AuNPs-AChE-ATC system to histidine, glucose, BaCl2, NaCl, KCl, Na2SO4, FeSO4, and fructose were studied according to the procedure described in section 2.4. The concentrations of carbaryl and the interfering substances were 20 μg/L and 2000 μg/L, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence recovered are not quenched again after adding the above substances, demonstrating that AChE activity was not inhibited by these interfering substances. As Fig. 6B displayed, these potential interfering substances do not cause any obvious changes in absorbance signals. These experimental results indicate that CQDs-AuNPs-AChE-ATC system exhibits excellent selectivity to carbaryl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in the analysis of water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the feasibility of the double signal system for the detection of carbaryl, we determined different concentrations of carbaryl spiked in lake water and tap water. The water samples were pretreated via filtration through a PES membrane (filter unit is 0.22 μm) to remove insoluble compounds. As expected, no carbaryl was detected in the water sample. Under the standard addition method, the recoveries of the dual-signal sensing ranged from 95.0% to 106.2% (Table 2 ). These results suggested that the proposed method was reliable for the assay of carbaryl in actual water samples.

In summary, a simple and sensitive dual-signaling (colorimetric and fluorometric) method relying on AuNPs-based IFE on fluorescence of CQDs for carbaryl detection was established, in which AuNPs served as both fluorescence quencher and colorimetric probe. The detection mechanism was illustrated by using various characterizations including zeta potential measurements, TEM images, fluorescence and absorption spectra measurements. More importantly, the feasibility of the proposed method was verified by the determination of carbaryl in real water samples with satisfactory results.
The authors declare that they have no competing interests.
Yuye Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xiu Qin: Data curation. Chunling Yuan: Writing - review & editing. Rui Shi: Software, Validation. Yilin Wang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21567002), the Opening Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory for Agro-Environment and Agro-Product Safety (17-259-82) and the Dean Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery (GXKLB-201801) for their financial support.
